analogy: Think of "Biomolecules" through the chapter's own opening images: 104 BIOLOGY There is a wide diversity in living organisms in our biosphere. Now a question that arises in our minds is: Are all living organisms made of the same chemicals, i.e., elements and compounds? You have learnt in chemistry how elemental analysis is performed.... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 104 BIOLOGY, 9.1 HO W TO ANALYSE CHEMICAL COMPOSITION?, BIOMOLECULES, CHAPTER 9. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Biomolecules is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 104 BIOLOGY There is a wide diversity in living organisms in our biosphere.

The text explains that now a question that arises in our minds is: Are all living organisms made of the same chemicals, i.e., elements and compounds?

The text explains that you have learnt in chemistry how elemental analysis is performed.

The text explains that if we perform such an analysis on a plant tissue, animal tissue or a microbial paste, we obtain a list of elements like carbon, hydrogen, oxygen and several others and their respective content per unit mass of a living tissue.

The text explains that if the same analysis is performed on a piece of earth’s crust as an example of non-living matter, we obtain a similar list.

The text explains that what are the differences between the two lists?

The text explains that in absolute terms, no such differences could be made out.

The text explains that all the elements present in a sample of earth’s crust are also present in a sample of living tissue.

The text explains that however, a closer examination reveals that the relative abundance of carbon and hydrogen with respect to other elements is higher in any living organism than in earth’s crust (Table 9.1).

The text explains that we can continue asking in the same way, what type of organic compounds are found in living organisms?

The text explains that to get an answer, one has to perform a chemical analysis.

The text explains that we can take any living tissue (a vegetable or a piece of liver, etc.) and grind it in trichloroacetic acid (Cl3CCOOH) using a mortar and a pestle.

The text explains that if we were to strain this through a cheesecloth or cotton we would obtain two fractions.

The text explains that one is called the filtrate or more technically, the acid-soluble pool, and the second, the retentate or the acid-insoluble fraction.

The text explains that scientists have found thousands of organic compounds in the acid-soluble pool.

The text explains that bIOMOLECULES CHAPTER 9 9.1 How to Analyse Chemical Composition?

The text explains that 9.2 Primary and Secondary Metabolites 9.3 Biomacromolecules 9.4 Proteins 9.5 Polysaccharides 9.6 Nucleic Acids 9.7 Structure of Proteins 9.8 Enzymes

The text explains that analytical techniques, when applied to the compound give us an idea of the molecular formula and the probable structure of the compound.

The text explains that therefore elemental analysis gives elemental composition of living tissues in the form of hydrogen, oxygen, chlorine, carbon etc. while analysis for compounds gives an idea of Element % Weight of Earth’s crust Human body Hydrogen (H) 0.14 9.5 Carbon (C) 0.03 18.5 Oxygen (O) 46.6 65.0 Nitrogen (N) very little 3.3 Sulphur (S) 0.03 0.3 Sodium (Na) 2.8 0.2 Calcium (Ca) 3.6 1.5 Magnesium (Mg) 2.1 0.1 Silicon (Si) 27.7 negligible * Adapted from CNR Rao, Understanding Chemistry, Universities Press, Hyderabad.

The text explains that hence in solutions of different pH, the structure of amino acids changes.

The text explains that some tissues especially the neural tissues have lipids with more complex structures.

The text explains that 108 BIOLOGY 9.2 PRIMARY AND SECONDARY METABOLITES The most exciting aspect of chemistry deals with isolating thousands of compounds, small and big, from living organisms, determining their structure and if possible synthesising them.

The text explains that for reasons that are given in section 9.10, we can call these biomolecules as ‘metabolites’.

The text explains that while primary metabolites have identifiable functions and play known roles in normal physiologial processes, we do not at the moment, understand the role or functions of all the ‘secondary metabolites’ in host organisms.

The text explains that in the later chapters and years you will learn more about this.

The text explains that 9.3 BIOMACROMOLECULES There is one feature common to all those compounds found in the acid soluble pool.

The text explains that for this very reason, biomolecules, i.e., chemical compounds found in living organisms are of two types.

The text explains that bIOMOLECULES 109 Component % of the total cellular mass Water 70-90 Proteins 10-15 Carbohydrates 3 Lipids 2 Nucleic acids 5-7 Ions 1 TABLE 9.4 Average Composition of Cells compounds and are present not only as such but also arranged into structures like cell membrane and other membranes.

The text explains that when we grind a tissue, we are disrupting the cell structure.

The text explains that hence, dietary proteins are the source of essential amino acids.

The text explains that in a polysaccharide chain (say glycogen), the right end is called the reducing end and the left end is called the non-reducing end.

The text explains that 9.7 STRUCTURE OF PROTEINS Proteins, as mentioned earlier, are heteropolymers containing strings of amino acids.

The text explains that structure of molecules means different things in different contexts.

The text explains that in inorganic chemistry, the structure invariably refers to the molecular formulae (e.g., NaCl, MgCl2, etc.).

The text explains that organic chemists always write a two dimensional view of the molecules while representing the structure of the molecules (e.g., benzene, naphthalene, etc.).

The text explains that physicists conjure up the three dimensional views of molecular structures while biologists describe the protein structure at four levels.

The text explains that the sequence of amino acids i.e., the positional information in a protein – which is the first amino acid, which is second, and so on – is called the primary structure (Figure 9.3 a) of a protein.

The text explains that a protein is imagined as a line, the left end represented by the first amino acid and the right end represented by the last amino

The text explains that in proteins, only right handed helices are observed.

The text explains that other regions of the protein thread are folded into other forms in what is called the secondary structure (Fig.

The text explains that in addition, the long protein chain is also folded upon itself like a hollow woolen ball, giving rise to the tertiary structure (Fig.

The text explains that the manner in which these individual folded polypeptides or subunits are arranged with respect to each other (e.g. linear string of spheres, spheres arranged one upon each other in the form of a cube or plate etc.) is the architecture of a protein otherwise called the quaternary structure of a protein (Fig.

The text explains that an enzyme like any protein has a primary structure, i.e., amino acid sequence of the protein.

The text explains that an enzyme like any protein has the secondary and the tertiary structure.

The text explains that when you look at a tertiary structure Figure 9.3 Various levels of Protein Structure (a) Primary (b) Secondary (d) Quaternary Hydrogen bond Disulphide bond Beta– pleated sheet Polypeptide Tertiary Alpha – Helix (c)

The text explains that inorganic catalysts work efficiently at high temperatures and high pressures, while enzymes get damaged at high temperatures (say above 40°C).

The text explains that however, enzymes isolated from organisms who normally live under extremely high temperatures (e.g., hot vents and sulphur springs), are stable and retain their catalytic power even at high temperatures (upto 80°-90°C).

The text explains that what is meant by tertiary structure of proteins?

The text explains that find and write down structures of 10 interesting small molecular weight biomolecules.

The text explains that find if there is any industry which manufactures the compounds by isolation.

The text explains that find out and make a list of proteins used as therapeutic agents.

The text explains that find other applications of proteins (e.g., Cosmetics etc.) 5.

Source: NCERT Class 11 Biology, Chapter 9: Biomolecules

prelims_traps: Do not treat 104 BIOLOGY and 9.1 HO W TO ANALYSE CHEMICAL COMPOSITION? as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Biomolecules; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Biomolecules through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 104 BIOLOGY and 9.1 HO W TO ANALYSE CHEMICAL COMPOSITION? to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
